projects.title: Personal Website
projects.shortDescription: This website you're on. A digital home for my thoughts, projects, and adventures.
projects.description: A static personal website evolving into a structured content, product, and agent-readable platform.
projects.whatItIs: A personal archive for essays, projects, books, tools, adventures, and useful notes.
projects.whyIMadeIt: I wanted a durable home for the things I am learning and collecting instead of scattering them across social platforms.
projects.whatILearned: Clear information architecture matters more than adding another page.
projects.nextStep: Tighten the first-visitor path, keep improving project proof, and replace placeholder visuals with owned artifacts.
projects.documentaryLead: This is the live container for the whole archive. The project is less about having a website and more about learning how to make a personal operating system legible to other people.
projects.currentQuestion: How do I make the first five minutes feel like a guided tour instead of a pile of interesting rooms?

proofArtifacts.label: Shipped
proofArtifacts.value: Navigation, search, Field Notes, books, essays, projects, products, adventures, skills, and agent-readable API surfaces.

proofArtifacts.label: Tension
proofArtifacts.value: The archive has more depth than the homepage currently explains.

buildLog.title: Reframed Projects as a build documentary
buildLog.note: Moved project pages away from static portfolio cards and toward current questions, evidence, and dated progress notes.

buildLog.title: Structured the site as a living archive
buildLog.note: Generated pages, metadata, sitemaps, and API outputs so the site can scale without every new thought becoming a hand-edited page.

projects.imageAlt: Mock of the personal website with navigation and project cards
projects.notes:
This is the unscripted thinking layer for the project. Half the value of the site comes from the constraint of needing to write down what's on each page in a way that survives a stranger's first read.

Open question I keep returning to: how much of the homepage should be static "this is who I am" vs. live "this is what I'm thinking about right now"? Right now it leans static because that's easier to ship.

commentary.title: Detail pages live
commentary.body: Projects, movies, and podcasts now each have their own page with a notes section. Cards on the listing pages link straight in instead of opening a zoom modal.

projects.title: Newsletter
projects.shortDescription: A weekly-ish note sharing useful ideas, books, tools, questions, and experiments from the archive.
projects.description: A monthly newsletter for useful things before they become polished essays.
projects.whatItIs: A short email for ideas, objects, books, questions, and experiments worth keeping.
projects.whyIMadeIt: Some useful things are too small for essays but too valuable to disappear into private notes.
projects.whatILearned: A newsletter needs a clear promise before it needs volume.
projects.nextStep: Publish a sample issue and turn the archive page into the canonical signup surface.
projects.documentaryLead: This is the lightweight publishing channel for things before they become polished essays. It should feel like a useful note from the archive, not a content machine.
projects.currentQuestion: What is the smallest note format that is consistently worth opening?

proofArtifacts.label: Promise
proofArtifacts.value: One useful idea, object, book, question, or experiment from the archive.

proofArtifacts.label: Constraint
proofArtifacts.value: It has to be short enough to send and useful enough to keep.

buildLog.title: Kept the scope small
buildLog.note: The next version needs a sample issue before it needs a cadence.

projects.imageAlt: Open field notebook page with handwritten lines and a pen
